entries.title: Calculus Fundamental Theorem
entries.content: The theorem linking differentiation and integration, showing they are inverse operations.
entries.formula: d/dx (integral from a to x of f(t) dt) = f(x)
entries.example: Evaluating integral of x^2 from 1 to 3 as (27/3) - (1/3)

entries.title: Cartesian Coordinates
entries.content: A coordinate system specifying each point uniquely in a plane by a pair of numerical coordinates.
entries.example: Point (3, -2) in the plane

entries.title: Cartesian Product
entries.content: The set of all ordered pairs where the first element is from set A and the second is from set B.
entries.formula: A x B = {(a, b) : a in A, b in B}

entries.title: Cauchy-Schwarz Inequality
entries.content: An inequality relating the inner product of two vectors to their individual norms.
entries.example: Dot product of [1, 2] and [3, 4] is less than product of their lengths

entries.title: Cauchy's Integral Formula
entries.content: A central statement in complex analysis showing that a holomorphic function on a disk is completely determined by its values on the boundary.
entries.example: Evaluating contour integrals of complex functions

entries.title: Cayley-Hamilton Theorem
entries.content: A theorem in linear algebra stating that every square matrix satisfies its own characteristic equation.
entries.example: Substituting matrix A into characteristic polynomial results in zero matrix

entries.title: Central Limit Theorem
entries.content: In probability theory, the distribution of sample means approaches a normal distribution as sample size grows, regardless of shape.
entries.example: Averages of 100 die rolls follow a bell curve

entries.title: Central Tendency
entries.content: A single value that attempts to describe a set of data by identifying the central position within that data.
entries.example: Reporting the median salary of a company

entries.title: Centroid of Triangle
entries.content: The point where the three medians of a triangle intersect, representing the geometric center.
entries.example: Centroid of triangle with vertices (0,0), (6,0), and (0,6) is (2,2)

entries.title: Chain Rule
entries.content: A formula for computing the derivative of the composition of two or more functions.
entries.example: Derivative of sin(x^2) is cos(x^2) * 2x

entries.title: Characteristic Polynomial
entries.content: A polynomial associated with a square matrix, whose roots are the eigenvalues of that matrix.
entries.example: Finding eigenvalues of a 2x2 matrix

entries.title: Chebyshev's Inequality
entries.content: A theorem guaranteeing that in any probability distribution, nearly all values are close to the mean.
entries.example: At least 75% of data lies within 2 standard deviations

entries.title: Chi-Square Distribution
entries.content: A continuous probability distribution of the sum of squares of independent standard normal random variables.
entries.example: Used extensively in hypothesis testing and contingency tables

entries.title: Chi-Square Test
entries.content: A statistical test used to determine if there is a significant difference between observed and expected frequencies.
entries.example: Testing if a coin is fair by counting heads and tails

entries.title: Chord of Circle
entries.content: A straight line segment whose endpoints both lie on a circle.
entries.example: A diameter is the longest possible chord of a circle

entries.title: Circle Equation
entries.content: The algebraic equation representing all points at a fixed distance from a center point.
entries.example: Circle centered at (1, 2) with radius 5

entries.title: Circular Permutation
entries.content: The number of unique ways to arrange n distinct objects in a circle.
entries.example: Arranging 5 people around a round dining table in 24 ways

entries.title: Circumference
entries.content: The linear distance around the outside edge of a circle.
entries.example: Circumference of a wheel with diameter 10 is 10*pi

entries.title: Circumscribed Circle
entries.content: A circle that passes through all the vertices of a polygon.
entries.example: Drawing a circle exactly enclosing a triangle

entries.title: Closed Set
entries.content: A set whose complement is an open set, meaning it contains all its limit points.
entries.formula: S contains all x such that x is limit point of S
entries.example: The closed interval [0, 1] contains 0 and 1

entries.title: Coefficient of Determination
entries.content: R-squared, the proportion of variance in the dependent variable that is predictable from the independent variable.
entries.example: R-squared of 0.85 indicates good model fit

entries.title: Coefficient of Variation
entries.content: A standardized measure of dispersion of a probability distribution, defined as standard deviation over mean.
entries.example: Comparing variability between salary data in USD and EUR

entries.title: Cofactor Matrix
entries.content: A matrix formed by replacing each element of a square matrix with its cofactor.
entries.example: Used in calculating the inverse of a matrix

entries.title: Combinations
entries.content: The selection of items from a larger group where the order of selection does not matter.
entries.example: Choosing a committee of 3 students from a class of 10

entries.title: Commutative Group
entries.content: Also known as an Abelian group, where the operation commutes.
entries.example: Real numbers under addition

entries.title: Commutative Property
entries.content: The order of numbers in addition or multiplication does not change the result.

entries.title: Compact Space
entries.content: A topological space where every open cover has a finite subcover.
entries.formula: Every open cover U_i has finite subcover U_1...U_n
entries.example: Closed and bounded subsets of R^n are compact

entries.title: Complement of Event
entries.content: The event that the original event does not occur in a probability experiment.
entries.example: If chance of rain is 30%, chance of no rain is 70%

entries.title: Complement of Set
entries.content: The set of elements in the universal set that are not in the given set.
entries.formula: A' = {x in U : x not in A}
entries.example: Universal set integers, A is evens, A' is odds

entries.title: Complementary Angles
entries.content: Two angles whose sum is exactly 90 degrees (or pi/2 radians).
entries.example: Angles of 35 and 55 degrees are complementary

entries.title: Complex Conjugate
entries.content: A pair of complex numbers with equal real parts and imaginary parts of equal magnitude but opposite signs.
entries.formula: z* = x - i*y for z = x + i*y
entries.example: Conjugate of 3 + 4i is 3 - 4i

entries.title: Complex Number
entries.content: A number that can be expressed in the form x + iy, where x and y are real numbers, and i is the imaginary unit.

entries.title: Complex Plane
entries.content: A geometric representation of complex numbers as points in a two-dimensional Cartesian plane.
entries.example: Plotting 3 + 2i at coordinates (3, 2)

entries.title: Composite Function
entries.content: An operation where the output of one function becomes the input of another function.
entries.example: If f(x)=x^2 and g(x)=x+1, then f(g(x))=(x+1)^2

entries.title: Composite Number
entries.content: A positive integer greater than 1 that has a positive divisor other than 1 and itself.
entries.formula: n = a * b where a, b > 1
entries.example: 6 is composite because 6 = 2 * 3

entries.title: Compound Interest
entries.content: Interest calculated on the initial principal and also on the accumulated interest of previous periods.
entries.example: Earning interest on savings account balance monthly

entries.title: Conditional Probability
entries.content: The probability of event A occurring, given that event B has already occurred.
entries.example: Probability of drawing a spade given it is a black card

entries.title: Conic Sections
entries.content: Curves obtained by intersecting a circular cone with a flat plane: circle, ellipse, parabola, hyperbola.
entries.formula: Ax^2 + Bxy + Cy^2 + Dx + Ey + F = 0
entries.example: Orbit of a planet is an ellipse

entries.title: Conjugate Transpose
entries.content: The matrix obtained by taking the transpose and then the complex conjugate of each entry.
entries.example: Conjugate transpose of a complex matrix

entries.title: Continuity of Function
entries.content: A function is continuous if a small change in the input results in a small change in the output, with no sudden jumps.
entries.example: f(x) = x^2 is continuous everywhere
